title: Charmed Life — 1 (Fragment 5)
sourceText:
Living with Mrs. Sharp, Gwendolen seemed to expand. Her hair seemed brighter gold, her eyes deeper blue, and her whole manner was glad and confident. Perhaps Cat contracted a little to make room for her—he did not know. Not that he was unhappy. Mrs. Sharp was quite as kind to him as she was to Gwendolen. Town Councillors and their wives called several times a week and patted him on the head in the parlor. They sent him and Gwendolen to the best school in Wolvercote. Cat was happy there. The only drawback was that Cat was left-handed, and schoolmasters always punished him if they caught him writing with his left hand. But they did that at all the schools Cat had been to, and he was used to it. He had dozens of friends. All the same, at the heart of everything, he felt lost and lonely. So he clung to Gwendolen, because she was the only family he had.

Gwendolen was often rather impatient with him, though usually she was too busy and happy to be downright cross. “Just leave me alone, Cat,” she would say. “Or else.” Then she would pack exercise books into a music case and hasten next door for a lesson with Mr. Nostrum.

Mr. Nostrum was delighted to teach Gwendolen for the letters. Mrs. Sharp gave him one every term for a year, starting with the last. “Not all at once, in case he gets greedy,” she said. “And we’ll give him the best last.”

Gwendolen made excellent progress. Such a promising witch was she, indeed, that she skipped the First Grade Magic exam and went straight on to the Second. She took the Third and Fourth grades together just after Christmas and, by the following summer, she was starting on Advanced Magic. Mr. Nostrum regarded her as his favorite pupil—he told Mrs. Sharp so over the wall—and Gwendolen always came back from her lessons with him pleased and golden and glowing. She went to Mr. Nostrum two evenings a week, with her magic case under her arm, just as many people might go to music lessons. In fact, music lessons were what Mrs. Sharp put Gwendolen down as having, on the accounts she kept for the Town Council. Since Mr. Nostrum never got paid, except by the letters, Cat thought this was rather dishonest of Mrs. Sharp.

“I have to put something by for my old age,” Mrs. Sharp told him crossly. “I don’t get much for myself out of keeping you, do I? And I can’t trust your sister to remember me when she’s grown-up and famous. Oh dear me no—I’ve no illusions about that!”

Cat knew Mrs. Sharp was probably right. He was a little sorry for her, for she had certainly been kind, and he knew by now that she was not a very good witch herself. The Certified Witch which the notice in Mrs. Sharp’s parlor window claimed her to be was, in fact, the very lowest qualification. People only came to Mrs. Sharp for charms when they could not afford the three Accredited Witches farther down the street. Mrs. Sharp eked out her earnings by acting as an agent for Mr. Larkins at the junk shop. She got him Exotic Supplies—that is to say, the stranger ingredients needed for spells—from as far away as London. She was very proud of her contacts in London. “Oh yes,” she often said to Gwendolen, “I’ve got the contacts, I have. I know those that can get me a pound of dragons’ blood any time I ask, for all it’s illegal. While you have me, you’ll never be in need.”

words.definition: Appeared to be; gave the impression of being.
words.example: Her hair seemed brighter gold.
words.exampleText: Her hair seemed brighter gold.

words.definition: Feeling or showing certainty about something.
words.example: Her whole manner was glad and confident.
words.exampleText: Her whole manner was glad and confident.

words.definition: To become smaller or shrink.
words.example: Cat contracted a little to make room for her.
words.exampleText: Cat contracted a little to make room for her.

words.definition: A disadvantage or problem that makes something less appealing.
words.example: The only drawback was that Cat was left-handed.
words.exampleText: The only drawback was that Cat was left-handed.

words.definition: Using the left hand more naturally than the right.
words.example: Cat was left-handed and punished for writing with his left hand.
words.exampleText: Cat was left-handed and punished for writing with his left hand.

words.definition: To cause someone to suffer for an offense or fault.
words.example: Schoolmasters always punished him if they caught him writing with his left hand.
words.exampleText: Schoolmasters always punished him if they caught him writing with his left hand.

words.definition: Groups of twelve; many.
words.example: He had dozens of friends.
words.exampleText: He had dozens of friends.

words.definition: Feeling sad because of having no friends or company.
words.example: He felt lost and lonely at heart.
words.exampleText: He felt lost and lonely at heart.

words.definition: Held tightly onto someone or something.
words.example: He clung to Gwendolen, because she was the only family he had.
words.exampleText: He clung to Gwendolen, because she was the only family he had.

words.definition: Easily annoyed by delays or difficulties.
words.example: Gwendolen was often rather impatient with him.
words.exampleText: Gwendolen was often rather impatient with him.

words.definition: Annoyed or angry.
words.example: Usually she was too busy and happy to be downright cross.
words.exampleText: Usually she was too busy and happy to be downright cross.

words.definition: To hurry or move quickly.
words.example: She would pack exercise books and hasten next door for a lesson.
words.exampleText: She would pack exercise books and hasten next door for a lesson.

words.definition: Feeling very pleased or happy.
words.example: Mr. Nostrum was delighted to teach Gwendolen.
words.exampleText: Mr. Nostrum was delighted to teach Gwendolen.

words.definition: A fixed period of time for school or university classes.
words.example: Mrs. Sharp gave him one every term for a year.
words.exampleText: Mrs. Sharp gave him one every term for a year.

words.definition: Having an intense and selfish desire for something, especially wealth or power.
words.example: In case he gets greedy, she said.
words.exampleText: In case he gets greedy, she said.

words.definition: Showing signs of future success.
words.example: Such a promising witch was she.
words.exampleText: Such a promising witch was she.

words.definition: To go past or avoid something.
words.example: She skipped the First Grade Magic exam.
words.exampleText: She skipped the First Grade Magic exam.

words.definition: Far on or ahead in development or progress.
words.example: She was starting on Advanced Magic.
words.exampleText: She was starting on Advanced Magic.

words.definition: Considered or thought of in a specified way.
words.example: Mr. Nostrum regarded her as his favorite pupil.
words.exampleText: Mr. Nostrum regarded her as his favorite pupil.

words.definition: Showing warmth or brightness.
words.example: Gwendolen always came back golden and glowing from her lessons.
words.exampleText: Gwendolen always came back golden and glowing from her lessons.

words.definition: Not truthful or lying; untrustworthy.
words.example: Cat thought this was rather dishonest of Mrs. Sharp.
words.exampleText: Cat thought this was rather dishonest of Mrs. Sharp.

words.definition: False ideas or beliefs about something.
words.example: She said she had no illusions about her sister being famous.
words.exampleText: She said she had no illusions about her sister being famous.

words.definition: Officially recognized or guaranteed as having met certain standards.
words.example: The Certified Witch notice claimed Mrs. Sharp was certified.
words.exampleText: The Certified Witch notice claimed Mrs. Sharp was certified.

words.definition: Officially authorized or recognized.
words.example: There were three Accredited Witches farther down the street.
words.exampleText: There were three Accredited Witches farther down the street.

words.definition: To increase or supplement with difficulty or effort.
words.example: Mrs. Sharp eked out her earnings by acting as an agent for Mr. Larkins.
words.exampleText: Mrs. Sharp eked out her earnings by acting as an agent for Mr. Larkins.

words.definition: Unusual or foreign ingredients needed for spells.
words.example: She got him Exotic Supplies for spells from London.
words.exampleText: She got him Exotic Supplies for spells from London.

words.definition: Forbidden by law.
words.example: Dragons’ blood was illegal to obtain.
